exercises.title: Identify the lost update race condition
exercises.scenario: Concurrent reads and writes without locking cause lost updates.
exercises.expected_solution:
Correct balances: ACC1 = ₹200 (1000 - 500 - 300), ACC2 = +₹500, ACC3 = +₹300

Lost update scenario:
  T=0: Thread A reads ACC1.balance = 1000
  T=0: Thread B reads ACC1.balance = 1000 (concurrent read!)
  T=1: Thread A saves ACC1.balance = 500 (1000-500)
  T=1: Thread B saves ACC1.balance = 700 (1000-300) ← OVERWRITES Thread A!

Actual broken result:
  ACC1 = ₹700 (Thread B's write wins)
  ACC2 = ₹500 (correct)
  ACC3 = ₹300 (correct)
  ACC1 lost ₹300 — money CREATED from nowhere!

Fix options:
  1. Optimistic: @Version on Account — Thread B fails → retries with fresh balance=500
  2. Pessimistic: SELECT FOR UPDATE on ACC1 — Thread B waits while Thread A completes
  3. Isolation: SERIALIZABLE level — detects write-write conflict

test_cases.expected:
Correct balances: ACC1 = ₹200 (1000 - 500 - 300), ACC2 = +₹500, ACC3 = +₹300

Lost update scenario:
  T=0: Thread A reads ACC1.balance = 1000
  T=0: Thread B reads ACC1.balance = 1000 (concurrent read!)
  T=1: Thread A saves ACC1.balance = 500 (1000-500)
  T=1: Thread B saves ACC1.balance = 700 (1000-300) ← OVERWRITES Thread A!

Actual broken result:
  ACC1 = ₹700 (Thread B's write wins)
  ACC2 = ₹500 (correct)
  ACC3 = ₹300 (correct)
  ACC1 lost ₹300 — money CREATED from nowhere!

Fix options:
  1. Optimistic: @Version on Account — Thread B fails → retries with fresh balance=500
  2. Pessimistic: SELECT FOR UPDATE on ACC1 — Thread B waits while Thread A completes
  3. Isolation: SERIALIZABLE level — detects write-write conflict

hints: Both threads read the same initial balance (1000) before either writes
Second write overwrites first: Thread B saves 700, erasing Thread A's 500
Lost ₹300: money materializes from thin air (or disappears in reverse)

exercises.title: Add @Version to an @Entity
exercises.scenario: @Version tells Hibernate to check the version field on every UPDATE.
exercises.instructions: Fill in the blanks to add optimistic locking to the BankAccount entity.

blanks.hint: JPA annotation for optimistic locking field

hints: @Version: one annotation on an int/Integer/Long/Timestamp field
Hibernate: adds AND version=? to every UPDATE for this entity
Spring wraps Hibernate's exception: ObjectOptimisticLockingFailureException

exercises.title: Classify transaction isolation anomalies
exercises.scenario: Know which anomaly each isolation level prevents.
exercises.instructions:
For each scenario, identify: (1) the anomaly type, (2) minimum isolation level to prevent it:

A: Transaction A reads balance=1000. Transaction B deducts 500 and commits. Transaction A reads balance=500. (Two different values in same transaction)

B: Transaction A reads uncommitted data from Transaction B. Transaction B rolls back.

C: Transaction A counts orders (5 rows). Transaction B inserts a new order and commits. Transaction A counts orders again (6 rows).

D: Transaction A reads and changes a record. Transaction B reads the same uncommitted change.
hints: Dirty read: reading UNCOMMITTED changes from another transaction
Non-repeatable read: same row, different values, within one transaction
Phantom read: different NUMBER of rows (new rows inserted by concurrent transaction)

exercises.title: Implement optimistic locking with @Version
exercises.scenario: Add @Version and handle OptimisticLockException in the service.
exercises.instructions:
Write:
1. Ticket @Entity with @Version int version
2. TicketRepository (standard JpaRepository)
3. TicketService.bookTicket(Long id) that:
   - Loads ticket
   - Returns SOLD_OUT if available <= 0
   - Decrements available and saves
4. Show the SQL Hibernate generates with @Version
hints: @Version on int field: Hibernate adds AND version=? to UPDATE SQL
0 rows affected → Spring throws ObjectOptimisticLockingFailureException
Service must be @Transactional for Hibernate to manage the update

exercises.title: @Retryable for OptimisticLockException
exercises.scenario: Spring Retry automatically retries on optimistic lock conflicts.
exercises.instructions:
Add @Retryable to TicketService.bookTicket():
1. Import spring-retry dependency (pom.xml snippet)
2. @EnableRetry on @SpringBootApplication
3. @Retryable: catch OptimisticLockException + ObjectOptimisticLockingFailureException
4. maxAttempts=3, backoff: delay=50ms, multiplier=2.0
5. @Recover method for when all retries are exhausted
hints: @Retryable value: list both exception types (Spring wraps Hibernate's exception)
@Backoff(delay=50, multiplier=2.0): exponential backoff reduces thundering herd
@Recover: called when all retries exhausted — same method signature but Exception first

exercises.title: Pessimistic locking with @Lock PESSIMISTIC_WRITE
exercises.scenario: SELECT ... FOR UPDATE locks the row for the duration of the transaction.
exercises.instructions:
Write:
1. InventoryItem @Entity (id, sku, stockQty) — NO @Version needed
2. InventoryRepository: findBySkuWithLock() using @Lock(PESSIMISTIC_WRITE)
3. InventoryService.reserve(sku, qty): loads with lock, checks quantity, decrements
4. Show the SQL that @Lock generates
5. What happens to Thread B when Thread A holds the lock?
hints: @Lock(PESSIMISTIC_WRITE) → SELECT ... FOR UPDATE in SQL
Must be inside @Transactional: lock held until transaction commits
Thread B waits at FOR UPDATE — no concurrency until lock is released

exercises.title: @DataJpaTest: verify @Version detects conflict
exercises.scenario: Simulate a version conflict in a test to verify optimistic locking works.
exercises.instructions:
Write a @DataJpaTest that simulates an optimistic lock conflict:
1. Save a Product with @Version
2. Load it in two separate 'views' (simulating two threads)
3. Modify and save the first view (version becomes 2)
4. Try to save the second view (still version=1) → expect ObjectOptimisticLockingFailureException
5. Verify: only the first update succeeded (DB has correct state)
hints: testEm.flush() + testEm.clear(): forces SQL execution and clears L1 cache
Force stale version: entity.version=0 simulates loading before concurrent update
assertThrows(ObjectOptimisticLockingFailureException.class, ...): verify conflict detected

exercises.title: Propagation.REQUIRES_NEW for audit logging
exercises.scenario: Audit log must be saved even if the main transaction rolls back.
exercises.instructions:
Write:
1. AuditLog @Entity (id, operation, entityId, userId, timestamp, success)
2. AuditService.log(operation, entityId, userId, success) with REQUIRES_NEW
3. OrderService.placeOrder() that:
   - Saves the order
   - If payment fails (throws RuntimeException), audit log STILL saves
4. @DataJpaTest (2 tests): order fails but audit log persists, order succeeds both persist
hints: REQUIRES_NEW: audit transaction commits independently of the outer transaction
RuntimeException: triggers rollback in @Transactional (default behavior)
testEm.flush(): forces SQL execution before clearing L1 cache

exercises.title: rollbackFor — checked exceptions and transaction rollback
exercises.scenario: @Transactional only rolls back RuntimeException by default — checked exceptions don't!
exercises.instructions:
Write a demonstration of the rollbackFor gap:
1. FileProcessingService.processAndSave(data) throws checked IOException
2. WITHOUT rollbackFor: record saved to DB even when IOException thrown
3. WITH rollbackFor=Exception.class: IOException triggers rollback
4. @DataJpaTest (2 tests): verify the different behaviors
hints: Default @Transactional: only RuntimeException and Error trigger rollback
Checked exceptions (IOException, SQLException) do NOT rollback by default
rollbackFor=Exception.class: includes ALL exceptions (checked and unchecked)

exercises.title: Optimistic vs pessimistic locking — when each wins
exercises.scenario: Choose the right locking strategy based on the use case.
exercises.instructions:
For each scenario, state: (1) optimistic or pessimistic locking, (2) why:

A: E-commerce product rating: 10,000 users rate a product daily. Conflicts are rare.
B: Concert ticket booking: 1 ticket left, 5,000 users click simultaneously.
C: Bank transfer: updating account balance — must be exact, errors unacceptable.
D: Shopping cart: user updates their own cart (no other users access it).
E: Airline seat reservation: flight at 80% capacity, 200 users booking simultaneously.
exercises.expected_solution:
A: OPTIMISTIC locking
  Low contention (10,000 users on thousands of products).
  Conflicts are rare (same user rating same product simultaneously is unlikely).
  Retry on conflict is acceptable — occasional re-try is cheap vs blocking 10,000 users.

B: PESSIMISTIC locking
  Extreme contention: 5,000 users competing for 1 item.
  Optimistic: 4,999 users get OptimisticLockException → all retry → more collisions!
  Pessimistic: serialized access — users queue, get definitive SOLD OUT immediately.
  Under ultra-high contention, optimistic + retry amplifies the problem.

C: PESSIMISTIC locking (or optimistic with very short retry)
  Financial correctness is critical. Lost update = money disappears.
  Pessimistic: guaranteed serial execution for each account.
  Alternative: optimistic + REQUIRES_NEW audit + careful retry.
  Most banks use pessimistic locking for balance updates.

D: Neither needed (or optimistic as a safeguard)
  User's own cart has no concurrent access from other users.
  Still worth @Version to detect tab-sync bugs (same user, two browser tabs).
  Optimistic with 0 retries acceptable here.

E: OPTIMISTIC locking
  200 users, 80% seats available → most seats uncongested.
  Conflicts rare — different users choosing different seats.
  @Retryable handles the occasional conflict (choose another seat).
  Pessimistic would serialize ALL seat selections → slow at scale.

test_cases.expected:
A: OPTIMISTIC locking
  Low contention (10,000 users on thousands of products).
  Conflicts are rare (same user rating same product simultaneously is unlikely).
  Retry on conflict is acceptable — occasional re-try is cheap vs blocking 10,000 users.

B: PESSIMISTIC locking
  Extreme contention: 5,000 users competing for 1 item.
  Optimistic: 4,999 users get OptimisticLockException → all retry → more collisions!
  Pessimistic: serialized access — users queue, get definitive SOLD OUT immediately.
  Under ultra-high contention, optimistic + retry amplifies the problem.

C: PESSIMISTIC locking (or optimistic with very short retry)
  Financial correctness is critical. Lost update = money disappears.
  Pessimistic: guaranteed serial execution for each account.
  Alternative: optimistic + REQUIRES_NEW audit + careful retry.
  Most banks use pessimistic locking for balance updates.

D: Neither needed (or optimistic as a safeguard)
  User's own cart has no concurrent access from other users.
  Still worth @Version to detect tab-sync bugs (same user, two browser tabs).
  Optimistic with 0 retries acceptable here.

E: OPTIMISTIC locking
  200 users, 80% seats available → most seats uncongested.
  Conflicts rare — different users choosing different seats.
  @Retryable handles the occasional conflict (choose another seat).
  Pessimistic would serialize ALL seat selections → slow at scale.

hints: Optimistic: low contention, rare conflicts, retry acceptable
Pessimistic: high contention, must serialize, blocking is acceptable
Rule: if conflict rate > 10%, consider pessimistic to avoid retry amplification

exercises.title: Pessimistic lock timeout — don't wait forever
exercises.scenario: Lock timeout prevents indefinite blocking under deadlock or slow transactions.
exercises.instructions:
Write a TicketRepository with pessimistic lock + 3-second timeout:
1. @Lock(PESSIMISTIC_WRITE)
2. @QueryHint: jakarta.persistence.lock.timeout=3000
3. TicketService.bookWithTimeout(): catches LockTimeoutException
4. Returns TIMEOUT result if lock can't be acquired in 3 seconds
5. Show what error is thrown when timeout expires
hints: jakarta.persistence.lock.timeout: milliseconds to wait for lock
LockTimeoutException: thrown when timeout expires without acquiring lock
Without timeout: deadlock = thread blocks forever = thread pool exhausts

exercises.title: Zerodha trade position — concurrent update with @Version + @Retryable
exercises.scenario: Multiple trade executions update the same portfolio position simultaneously.
exercises.instructions:
Write the complete Zerodha position update:
1. PortfolioPosition @Entity: userId, symbol, totalQty, avgPrice, @Version version
2. PositionRepository: findByUserIdAndSymbol + findForUpdate (pessimistic)
3. PositionService.applyTrade(): @Retryable(OptimisticLock, maxAttempts=5) + weighted avg price
4. @DataJpaTest (2 tests): two trades apply correctly, version increments on each
hints: Weighted average: (oldAvg * oldQty + tradePrice * tradeQty) / newTotalQty
BigDecimal.divide(): must specify scale and RoundingMode to avoid ArithmeticException
@Version increments on each save — version after 2 saves > version after 1 save

exercises.title: BookMyShow seat booking — pessimistic lock with @DataJpaTest
exercises.scenario: Seat booking requires pessimistic locking to prevent double-booking.
exercises.instructions:
Build BookMyShow seat booking:
1. Seat @Entity: row, col, eventId, booked boolean, @Version for extra safety
2. SeatRepository: findAvailable() + findByIdForUpdate() with PESSIMISTIC_WRITE
3. SeatBookingService: bookSeat(seatId, userId) with pessimistic lock
4. @DataJpaTest (3 tests): book a seat, booking same seat twice fails, unbooked count correct

All 3 tests pass.
exercises.starter_code:
// BookMyShow seat booking with pessimistic lock
// Seat @Entity + SeatRepository + SeatBookingService + @DataJpaTest 3 tests
hints: PESSIMISTIC_WRITE on findByIdForUpdate: SELECT FOR UPDATE prevents concurrent booking
@Transactional required: lock held until method returns
Second booking attempt: isBooked()=true after first commits → returns false

exercises.title: Deadlock prevention with consistent lock ordering
exercises.scenario: Deadlocks occur when transactions acquire locks in different orders.
exercises.instructions:
Two operations each lock accounts in different order:
- transferAtoB(accA, accB): locks accA then accB
- transferBtoA(accB, accA): locks accB then accA

1. Show the deadlock scenario (which thread blocks which)
2. Write a deadlock-free transfer by always locking in ascending ID order
3. Implement AccountTransferService.transfer(fromId, toId, amount) with consistent ordering
4. @DataJpaTest: two transfers in opposite directions don't deadlock

All tests pass.
hints: Deadlock: Thread A waits for B's lock, Thread B waits for A's lock → circular wait
Prevention: always lock resources in the SAME order across all transactions
Math.min/max: sort IDs so both threads always lock smaller ID first

exercises.title: Complete Razorpay payment processor — transactions synthesis
exercises.scenario: Production-grade payment processor with optimistic locking, audit, and retry.
exercises.instructions:
Build the complete Razorpay payment processor:
1. PaymentOrder @Entity: @Version, merchantId, amount, status (PENDING/CAPTURED/FAILED)
2. PaymentRepository: standard JpaRepository
3. AuditService with Propagation.REQUIRES_NEW
4. PaymentService.capture(orderId): @Retryable(OptimisticLock), captures payment, logs audit
5. PaymentService.refund(orderId): @Transactional(rollbackFor=Exception.class), @Retryable
6. @DataJpaTest (3 tests): capture success, duplicate capture fails (wrong status), audit saved on failure

All 3 tests pass.
exercises.starter_code: // Complete Razorpay payment processor — transactions synthesis
hints: @Retryable + @Transactional: Spring creates a new transaction for each retry attempt
REQUIRES_NEW audit: commits even if outer transaction (capture) fails or rolls back
capture() throws IllegalStateException for non-PENDING → not caught by OptimisticLock retry
